scenario: A team's BPMN diagrams have gateways labeled inconsistently -- some labels are questions, some are commands, some are blank. The team lead wants a naming convention for gateways.
question: Recommended naming conventions for BPMN gateways?

options.text: ". Each outgoing flow labeled with the answer ("Yes" / "No" / specific values). ". Outgoing flows labeled with their specific conditions. - Parallel (AND) split: typically unlabeled or descriptive ("Split for parallel processing"). - Event-Based split: descriptor -- "First event wins". Outgoing flows labeled with event types. - Joins (any type): typically unlabeled or minimal ("Merge"). Consistent across team; documented in BPMN style guide.

Without consistent conventions, diagrams from different team members or created at different times will be inconsistent, making them harder to read, review, and maintain. Exclusive (XOR) gateways with blank labels force readers to examine outgoing flow conditions to understand the decision point's purpose. Parallel gateways mixed with question-format labels create confusion about whether branches are conditional or concurrent. Consistent conventions -- documented in a team BPMN style guide and enforced during BPMN review -- significantly improve diagram readability and reduce misunderstanding.

Claiming that all gateway types should use the same label format is only partially correct in a limited interpretation. While some consistency across all gateways is better than no consistency, the optimal approach is to use label formats appropriate to each gateway type. Exclusive gateways communicate decisions, so question-format labels clarify their purpose. Parallel gateways perform unconditional splits, so question-format labels would be misleading. Event-based gateways describe waiting for competing events, requiring a different descriptive style. A single uniform label format across all gateway types loses the semantic differentiation that gateway-type-specific conventions provide.

Gateway labels in Camunda's Web Modeler and Desktop Modeler are manually entered by the modeler. The modeling tool does not automatically generate meaningful labels for gateways based on their type or connected flows. Teams must deliberately adopt and apply naming conventions for their gateways. Web Modeler may generate a default empty label or a placeholder, but semantically meaningful labels that follow question-format or descriptive conventions must be created and maintained by the process designers.

a.text: Correct. Per-gateway-type conventions clarify intent: Exclusive (XOR) split -- decision point: Gateway label: question form. "Approved?", "Customer tier?", "Order value > 1000?" Flow labels: the answer or value. "Yes" / "No"; "GOLD" / "SILVER" / "BRONZE"; ">1000" / "<=1000". Reader sees: question + answers → decision branching is obvious. Inclusive (OR) split -- conditional branches: Gateway label: descriptive or question. "Which validations apply?", "Applicable discounts?". Flow labels: specific condition / branch name. "Address check", "Credit score check", "ID verification". Reader sees: multiple branches may activate; conditions per branch. Parallel (AND) split -- concurrent branches: Gateway label: typically unlabeled (the split itself doesn't make a decision) or short descriptive ("Split for parallel processing"). Flow labels: optional descriptors of each parallel path's purpose. Reader sees: all branches activate concurrently. Event-Based split -- race condition: Gateway label: descriptor -- "First event wins" / "Race timeout vs response". Each outgoing event: clearly labelled by what it represents. Reader sees: pause-and-race semantic. Joins (all types): Typically unlabeled -- joins don't add decision logic. "Merge" if helpful for clarity in complex diagrams. Team-level: Document conventions in a BPMN style guide. Code review BPMN as you do code: enforce consistency. Inconsistency hurts readability; consistency aids understanding.

b.text: Incorrect. Option c) -- Partial. Same format doesn't fit all gateways.

findings.message: option text contains its own verdict/rationale
